**Attempt #3:**

**Modifier Equation for the P(*n*) Equation**

**06/06/2026 - 10/06/2026**

&emsp;My third attempt expanded upon the second $P(n)$ created in the second attempt. This time around, much of the focus was shifted away from the $S_{p}(n)$ equation and towards the $P(n)$ equation. I had the postulate that, perchance, there could be a multiplier equation created that accounts for the differences between the generated primes with $P(n)$ and the actual prime integers at $n$.

&emsp;By dividing the prime generated by $P(n)$ with the actual $n^{\text{th}}$ prime integer, I obtained the following table:

| $n$ | $P(n)$ | $A_{p}$ | $r$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 10 | 31 | 29 | $\frac{29}{31}$ |
| 20 | 74 | 71 | $\frac{71}{74}$ |
| 30 | 122 | 113 | $\frac{113}{122}$ |
| 40 | 174 | 173 | $\frac{173}{174}$ |
| 50 | 229 | 229 | $\frac{229}{229}$ |
| 60 | 285 | 281 | $\frac{281}{285}$ |
| 70 | 344 | 349 | $\frac{349}{344}$ |
| 80 | 404 | 409 | $\frac{409}{404}$ |
| 90 | 467 | 463 | $\frac{463}{467}$ |
| 100 | 531 | 541 | $\frac{541}{531}$ |
| ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 500 | 3725 | 3571 | $\frac{3571}{3725}$ |
| 1000 | 8610 | 7919 | $\frac{7919}{8610}$ |
| 2000 | 19894 | 17389 | $\frac{17389}{19894}$ |

&emsp;Running a regression analysis for the $r$ column, I get the following equation:

![](../images/Attempt_3/Attempt_3-Regress1.png)
![](data:image/png;base64,)


&emsp;Although the $R^2$ value is not exactly equal to 1 (indicative that the sinusoidal modifier equation created is not a perfect match to the values generated in the $r$ column), it's still a proof of concept. Using this proof of concept sinusoidal modifier equation created through the regression analysis, I formulated the following equation for $P(n)$:

$$P\left(n\right)=\left(0.0535953\cdot\sin\left(0.00508236n+0.58284\right)+0.949187\right)\cdot\left(\frac{1}{S_{p}\left(n\right)}-\frac{1}{S_{p}\left(n-1\right)}\right)$$

&emsp;Where:

$$S_{p}\left(n\right)=\frac{1}{2+\lfloor0.928914\left(n^{2.20778}\right)\rfloor}$$

&emsp;The following graph was generated using the sinusoidal modifier equation to the original $P(n)$ equation.

![](../images/Attempt_3/Attempt_3-Graph1.png)
![](data:image/png;base64,)


&emsp;As seen below, the new $P(n)$ equation generated is more accurate than the previous $P(n)$ equation generated by Attempt #2.

| $n$ | $P_{old}(n)$ | $P_{new}(n)$ | $A_p$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 500 | 3725 | 3539 | 3571 |
| 1000 | 8610 | 7905 | 7919 |
| 1500 | 14054 | 14047 | 12553 |
| 2000 | 19894 | 17850 | 17389 |
| 2500 | 26048 | 25647 | 22307 |
| 3000 | 32467 | 30606 | 27449 |

&emsp;Unfortunately, there still exists a vast difference between the primes generated by the $P(n)$ equation and the actual prime integers.

![](../images/Attempt_3/Attempt_3-Graph2.png)
![](data:image/png;base64,)


&emsp;A new approach should be taken with Attempt #4, straying away from the proposed approach assumed in Attempt #2. Although the sinusoidal modifier equation assisted in the correction of the $P(n)$ equation created in Attempt #2, forming an even closer approximation, it is clear from this attempt that the modifier was insufficient in fully correcting the primes generated by the $P(n)$ equation. So, perchance the creation of the $n^{\text{th}}$ prime integer formula should be reimagined entirely.
